Also from reading the earlier comments, I think there might be some confusion as to caching, hashability, and mutability.

So, nothing is truly immutable in Python (unless you implement your stuff in C), you can only approximate immutability by making it really hard to mutate (frozen dataclasses are a good example. They can be mutated, but it takes gymnastics).

Hashability implicitly requires immutability because otherwise the hash wouldnt be useful. Even the stdlib approximates mutability by asking if the class is hashable.

Hashes of objects with similar data aren't guaranteed to be stable across process runs. So they are only good for in-process memoization.

We frequently use frozen dataclasses because they provide hash semantics for us (and provide "deep" hashing semantics).

Now enter Pants. At the rule boundaries, the inputs/outputs are cached in process (pantsd, the daemon, specifically). That's why the daemon gets you the big boost when issuing multiple pants commands. It's reusing the same process which has a warm cache of objects with hashes we assume are stable.

We don't use this cache across multiple processes (so restarts of the daemon, turning off the daemon, or across machines with remote caching, or remote execution), again because they aren't stable across processes.

The speedup you see across processes comes from caching `Digest`s (hashes of files/directory trees) and `Process`es (the object responsible for sandboxing and running a process.

So that's why we "require" immutability of rule input/outputs. If something isn't hashable, that'd straight up be a nonstarter. Closures are hashable, but you get a different hash per closure, so it wouldn't be incorrect, you'd just skip out on in-process caching, which is obviously not a great thing.

Now a closure itself is immutable, but like-closures generate different hashes so the caching isn't incorrect, it's just useless.

The issue is if it's referencing a mutable variable. So that's why only allowing a frozen dataclass is the safest and best way forward. It is itself immutable, and can only hold references to immutable members.

Getting values from a QueryParams object discards all but the first value. Example query:
```
?session_id=2&ids=1&ids=2&ids=3
```
produces
```
dict(query_params) -> {"session_id": [2], "ids": [1, 2, 3]}
query_params -> {"session_id": 2, "ids": 1}
```
In the instance of session_id, we're not expecting mutlitple values, so calling `get` on QueryParams is fine. 
This would also work for the second line:
`dict(request.query_params).get("session_id")[0]`
but it would throw a (more vague) IndexError in case session_id was missing.

If you define the default value like that, it's going to be mutated for all future calls. Moreover the method calls to get the values won't happen on the first call but on the method declaration.

I honestly didn't know exactly how Cython works, and figured that I could simply reuse a single `.pyx` source.  And I was wrong...

`context.run` releases the GIL for the most part of its execution so it makes sense to run it on its own thread but there a few caveats:
* it's hard to scale the number of worker threads to match the load of the HTTP workers. For example, let's say an app uses gevent and can handle 100 concurrent requests with a single process, how many threads should we use?
* there is a small latency penalty induced by synchronisation (queuing the data & waiting for the result). We tried to use `concurrent.futures` but it was really slow on Python 2. Maybe there is another implementation than https://github.com/agronholm/pythonfutures but I didn't move forward on the experimentation.

Referencing the implementation of class OpenThreadTHCI in *OpenThread.py*, Harness calls `reset()` by executing `self.__sendCommand('factoryreset', expectEcho=False)`, which does not run `_cliReadLine()` after sending the `factoryreset` command.

In the case that there is residual data, it cannot be the output of any commands executed before in the current node as far as I know. It will never execute the next command when there is still more output by the previous commands. The only possibility is the output of asynchronous messages, such as receiving the data sent by other nodes using `udp send`, and other nodes joining the commissioner. OpenThreadTHCI can filter and ignore the asynchronous messages properly. It may read the residual data at the next startup and ignores it without bringing any trouble. Moreover, I think it will not harm the device responsiveness even there are errors, since the asynchronous messages are always valid and can be distinguished while synchronous messages have an ending of several fixed patterns.

In fact, I think the non-blocking `_cliReadLine()` before may be a redundant design. Because it never returns `None` in the blocking mode with a timeout during all the test cases conducted afterwards.

This is the point where I think I need to explain the intention and difference between this PR and #440.
In #440 the implementation is not advertising the registry to be used on the module level instance `responses.mock`. Instead it is implemented similar to `responses.mock.response_callback`, where all the warnings about the leakage of environment are equally valid for the current version of this library. However, in contrast to the module level `response_callback` which is just a reference to `None`, a module level reference to `registry` could make sense, assuming that the registry of the default mock is never swapped out.
So the difference is, that this PR tries to implement the registry functionality to be also used with the default mock, while #440 intents to provide the functionality only for local instances of `RequestsMock`.
Maybe it is just me who is scared about global (module level) instances, but note how much complexity is needed to restore the initial state of the default mock instance... What about a global reset function which just recreates a new instance of default mock with a well defined state? Of course, this reset function will also need to reset the global pointers to the new instance methods and attributes.

There's a bit of an impedance mismatch here for the dict argument in `agg`. An instance of `AggregateFn` already has its column selection hardcoded, while the mapping from column name to aggregations that we want to apply is basically mapping `column: str` --> `agg_cls: type[AggregateFn]`, and then we use that mapping to create concrete aggregation instances: `agg_cls(on=column)`. I.e., giving `{"col1": [Mean()]}` doesn't make sense for the current `AggregateFn` API, since the `on="col1"` selection needs to be given to `Mean` on construction, not after.

I can explore a different API/model for the aggregation functions so the column selection can be specified at call time, that could have some other advantages too!

As mentioned in the [design doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1OGkiOJ-mIxmgNNigO9E7XTCZxzXaj-36u1hQQnAjZgg/edit#), We made a few changes to the threading model:
> Adjust the sending thread model, if the preconfigured max length threshold is reached, the user thread returns an error immediately to indicate the remote call is failed due to backpressure. **If not, the user thread puts TaskSpec object into client queue and then post sending lambda to IO thread, and sent out by IO thread**.

Tansy solved this for coverage by having the generic boolean option `--run-coverage`, then having Pytest Coverage have specific options for the report type and where to write the report in the subsystem `pytest-coverage`: 

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fca3558b3142acdecb5fe5730e6c5d342482e45b/src/python/pants/backend/python/rules/pytest_coverage.py#L218-L238

Here, the boolean option is right, but you'll want to rename the option to something more generic like `save-results` or `write-results`. Also update the `help` message.'

Then, you could add options to https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/python/subsystems/pytest.py to allow specifying where those results should be saved. 

If you want to support multiple formats for those results, you could make that an enum option like Tansy did with report types. If you only care about supporting XML for now, leave off that option for the type of results.

Next bit: update `pytest_runner.py` to a) propagate the relevant flags to Pytest, which you already do; then b) capture the relevant folder, which it looks like you also now do.

For splitting up the resulting digest into its relevant pieces, you'll use `SnapshotSubset`:

```python
coverage_digest = await Get[Digest](SnapshotSubset(output_digest, PathGlobs(".coverage"))
results_digest = await Get[Digest](SnapshotSubset(output_digest, PathGlobs(test_results_file))
```

--

Final bit: hook up `test.py` to write the reports to the build root, if any. This needs to happen in the `@goal_rule` because it's a side effect. See below for how we do it with coverage:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fca3558b3142acdecb5fe5730e6c5d342482e45b/src/python/pants/core/goals/test.py#L291-L321

Your implementation can likely be substantially easier because a) we don't care about grouping related Results files, whereas Coverage needs this so that we can merge multiple individual results into one single report; and b), we don't have an option to open the results.

Your implementation will likely be closer to how we do `binary.py`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fca3558b3142acdecb5fe5730e6c5d342482e45b/src/python/pants/core/goals/binary.py#L114-L127

I'm in agreement that I don't love the `ABCWithLogger` base class and the semi-complex juggling that is required to make it work, but I'm in favor of it **over** having the following line all over our code.

```python
logger = cast(ExtendedDebugLogger, logging.getLogger('path.to.MyLogger')
```

If it was *only* the `cast` I might be ok with it but it's also that there is no guarantee that `logging.getLogger` will return an actual `ExtendedDebugLogger` since that depends on `logging.setLoggerClass` having been last called with `ExtendedDebugLogger`.

So my reasoning is, isolated complexity here to remove lots of fragile feeling code across most of our code.

However, in saying this, I realize there's an allowable 3rd option that we should consider.  We can write a `get_extended_debug_logger(name)` function which uses the following pattern from `eth-utils` to reliably return an `ExtendedDebugLogger` instance.

Manifest repo, as example `nrf-repo`.

> HEAD: bad (manifest: `allow-list`)
> ...
> HEAD\~25
> ...
> HEAD\~50: unknown (manifest: `white-list`)
> ...
> HEAD\~75
> ...
> HEAD\~100: good (manifest: `white-list`)

Starting a bisect:
```
git bisect start
git bisect bad
git bisect good HEAD~100
Bisecting: 49 revisions left to test after this (roughly 6 steps)
[HEAD~75] <text>
west update (this fails, cause latest `west` doesn't support `white-list` --> downgrade west)
pip install west==0.7.3
<test>
git bisect good
Bisecting: 24 revisions left to test after this (roughly 5 steps)
[HEAD~25] <text>
west update (this fails, cause old `west` doesn't support `allow-list` --> upgrade west)
pip install west==0.x.y
west update
<test>
git bisect bad
... --> maybe west needs to be downgraded this time, maybe not ?
```

you start to see how annoying this is to users, cause they won't know when a SHA that requires a different `west` version is needed, until `west update` fails.

Reasons imports are bad:
1. The imports people _tend_ to try to use in BUILD files break our caching model. e.g. reading a file, or reading an env var - the engine won't invalidate the BUILD file parse if these change, so they'll get stale results.
2. BUILD file parsing needs to be deterministic, and ideally should be relatively fast; it's hard to accidentally violate either without imports; imports make it much easier to violate these properties.

If people really need imports, they should write their code in a pants plugin, because we (at least try to) over-invalidate when those change.

Originally, there were some inconsistencies with the typing ([source](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/0c74f7da3a5bfeb60aadf0432ee8d892508c52a4/sdk/monitor/azure-monitor-query/azure/monitor/query/_models.py#L21)) where `columns` and `column_types` were `:vartype columns: list[str]` and `:vartype column_types: list[object]`. However, the constructor had:

```
self.columns = kwargs.pop("columns", None)  # type: Optional[str]
self.columns_types = kwargs.pop("column_types", None)  # type: Optional[Any]
```

In a recent typing update last month (https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/28175) where I added all the class attributes for typing, I opted to go with the typing used in the type-hint comments of the constructor.

However, now after looking closer at the expected return from the service API, `columns` is [required](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/dba6ed1f03bda88ac6884c0a883246446cc72495/specification/operationalinsights/data-plane/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/preview/2021-05-19_Preview/OperationalInsights.json#L374) in the response. This was why I removed the `Optional` for these, returning to the original `:vartype` typing. Changing the default value in the `kwargs.pop` operations from `None` to `[]` was an attempt to reconcile the fact that these attributes should not be `None`.

I hope that helps to explain my thinking.

The global conftest now removes all `_DEFAULT_FILES` so the default behavior in tests is that it won't find any of these files.

In `test_config.py` we have tests that expect `_DEFAULT_FILES` to match our production list, and have their own mocks that mock opening these files instead. So by reverting to this list in the module we let the existing test mocks take care of it. The override works kind of like python's LEGB for variables, the module level fixture overrides the root one.

Maybe a better way long-term would be to completely rework the tests in this module to mock things differently, but this resulted in the smallest diff.

If `counts` is an array, it needs to be a one-dimensional array of numbers, so

```python
if not type(counts).__module__.startswith("cupy."):
    counts = ak.nplike.numpy.asarray(counts)
```

will take any array-like into a NumPy or CuPy array. If `counts` is an Awkward Array, it will be (zero-copy) converted _if_ it is one-dimensional and raise an exception if it is not. This will handle any node types, not just IndexedOptionArray (and I think this handling here is not sufficiently general).

Or wait—do you have this because you want to allow and pass through missing values? That is, if there are any None values in your `counts` array, you want these to show up as None in the output? In that case, you can do

```python
if not type(counts).__module__.startswith("cupy."):
    counts = ak.operations.convert.to_numpy(counts, allow_missing=True)
    mask = ak.nplike.numpy.ma.getmask(counts)
    counts = ak.nplike.numpy.ma.filled(counts, 0)
else:
    mask = False
```

The first line in the `if` block returns a NumPy array or NumPy masked array, the second gets a boolean array of missing values or a single False if it's not a masked array, and the third line ensures that any masked values are filled with zero.

After creating the `offsets` and `out` as a ListOffsetArray64, you can then replace the `if mask_layout is not None` block with

```python
if mask is not False:
    out = ak.layout.ByteMaskedArray(ak.layout.Index8(mask.view(np.int8)), out, valid_when=False)
```

IndexedOptionArray is not the only way to make (or get) option-type data. Using `to_numpy` with `allow_missing=True` handles all of the cases, giving you a masked array, and since you have a masked array, the easiest way to use it is to make a ByteMaskedArray.

The [recommended practice](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/exceptions.html#c.PyErr_WriteUnraisable) for handling exceptions raised during execution of destructors is to use `PyErr_WriteUnraisable` passing the object being destroyed as it's argument.

This is simpler and more straightforward that trying to chain exceptions. The issue with chaining exceptions is that since I can only pass string error message to `raise_from`, we loose the traceback of the exception caused by `PyCapsule_Name`.
To turn `value` associated with this exception we need to call `PyObject_Repr` on it, which can raise additional exceptions. 

I am thus giving up on the chaining idea and will use `PyErr_WriteUnraisable` instead.

> I'm interpretting the `max_memory_usage` as a total cap for all potential processes spawned, instead of the max memory per-process.

That's correct.

> A max cap for all processes has potential when running pants in constrained environments and implementation-wise would mean calculating a max-memory per process factoring in parallelism and passing the flags `-Xmx{calculated_value}mb` and `-Xms{default_size}mb` to each nailgun instance such that they all can fit inside in the total cap given in the options.

So, there is one very important bit missing here, which is that: it's important for reproducibility to use a consistent max per-process heap size across all consumers of a repository. Otherwise, compilation and testing might fail on one machine, and succeed on another for a particular target. So the `child_process_default_memory_usage` value needs to be constant/static, and cannot be calculated.

> So for the cases in which there is not a max cap value given, then the pool size would be calculated based only on the parallelism and would not pass any `-Xmx...` flag to the JVM.

The first part is correct, but as to `-Xmx`: we should always pass the `child_process_default_memory_usage` as a templated `-Xmx` value, for the reason given above (reproducibility).

One of the reasons for this is because there have been many requests for implementing a meta-field that allows outputting the recursion depth (which we haven't implemented yet because of lack of support for this in OrientDB 2). If and when we implement this feature, one should be able to query for things like "all the cycles and cycle lengths that start and end at A, to max depth 7".

Unfortunately, recursion can definitely get quite expensive, even on the other backends. This is something the compiler can help with, when we get a chance to integrate query size estimation and automatic query paging, but in the end we have to have some amount of trust that the user knows what they are asking for. Even without cycles, the database can have very deep trees and the user can set a recursion depth that is way too high -- and the resulting query will be expensive no matter what.

I'd be open to allowing distinct-only recursion in the future as an addition, if there is a good use case for it and ideally if we are able to figure out a way to emit appropriate OrientDB code for it.

Inputs exist before invocation, at memoization time, in the same way that a normal arg or kwargs does, so id_for_memo can fully examine the input if it wants to (for example, I have prototyped taking a checksum of an input file so that checkpoints are invalidated if the contents of an input file changes - see the feature request at https://github.com/Parsl/parsl/issues/105).

In contrast to normal args, kwargs, and `inputs`, an `output` does not have content at memoization time. All that exists is the *name* of the output. (although there might be a file at the indicated name already, that will be overwritten and so is irrelevant)

So an id_for_memo which is more aware of #105-style content needs to know if it is looking at an input (in which case it can look at the content of the input) or an output (in which case it can only look at the name of the output).

Why do you remove that ?

If the field isn't always set on responses, for correctness we should have an explicit indicator to mark it as unset rather than leaving it at the default value of 0, since 0 is a valid CRC output: https://stackoverflow.com/a/49804094/1179226

The most idiomatic way to do that would be using the UInt32Value wrapper type (the well-known wrappers have special handling in JSON interop) and then we can leave it unset if no CRC was provided by the server:
https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/blob/master/src/google/protobuf/wrappers.proto

That said, up to you but I'd personally be inclined to leave it out unless we're confident that the server will populate it and that we think it's valuable for the client to check it (even in a deferred fashion).  As mentioned in your comment lower in the file, we have robust protection already from the full-file and per-chunk CRCs.

And actually, thinking about it just a bit further, I'm not sure that maintaining a running CRC provides any additional value over the per-chunk CRCs, because if a per-chunk CRC fails to detect corruption in that chunk, I think necessarily any further CRC calculations are not going to make a difference.  See the discussion in https://stackoverflow.com/a/23050676/1179226 which explains that for strings `a` and `b` you can calculate `crc(a + b)` using only `crc(a)`, `crc(b)` and `len(b)`.  So IIUC then if there's a chunk `b'` such that `crc(b') = crc(b)` and `len(b') = len(b)`, then we already know that `crc(a + b') = crc_combine(crc(a), crc(b'), len(b')) = crc_combine(crc(a), crc(b), len(b)) = crc(a + b)`, so the running CRC would also be identical.

I'm definitely not a CRC expert so maybe this analysis is flawed or there are ways to avoid that issue, but given that it seems like even if we did calculate it's possibly doing a lot of extra work for no extra protection, my inclination would be to omit it for now and we can always add it later.

We are distinguishing between the two use cases of the decorator-- with args and without args. This is a real pain for type-checkers because the logic of the decorator is totally different depending on which one you're using. Either:

- the first arg is a decorator param, and you have to return another decorator
- the first arg is the decorated function, and you return the decorated function

The best way to distinguish these cases is to use only named arguments for decorator parameters. I changed `whitelist_for_serdes` to enforce this using the single asterisk in the signature. It was already true of all-but-one callsite anyway (wherever the `serializer` parameter was passed, it was named).

IMO the logic is now much more straightforward. Previously the first arg was named `serializer`, and we were checking to see whether it was _actually_ a serializer (parameter case) or not (decorated class case). Now these two are no longer assigned to the same name `serializer`. `serializer` must be passed as a keyword arg, and is always actually a serializer. `__cls` is always the decorated class (`None` in the case we called the decorator with params).

Do you still think I need to add a comment explaining this?

BTW, since Dagster makes such heavy use of decorators that take params, IMO this pattern should be implemented throughout the codebase (decorator params are always keywords). Do we have any legitimate use case for passing positional args as decorator params? If that is truly the case, then the current implementation will generate invalid numbers.

Instead of calling this function recursively till the check sum does not equal to 10 (~9.1% chance of happening per attempt), I suggest using this algorithm instead when the check sum ends up being 10:

1. Increment/decrement a random digit by 1 (whichever is possible) 
2. Add/subtract the corresponding weight for that digit to the current check sum (which will be 10)
3. Apply modulo 11 to the new check sum, and the result will be the new check digit

For example, the number `123456789` will have a check sum of 10. If you want to alter the first digit from the left, say we increment it by 1, our number will become 223456789. The first digit's weight is 6, and since we incremented the first digit, we add it such that new check digit will be `(10 + 6) % 11 = 5`, so the new NIP will be `2234567895`.

The `config/mac.h` contains only a default definition for the config options. User of OT can choose to override these default values from build system definition or through specifying a project specific config header file (`OPENTHREAD_PROJECT_CORE_CONFIG_FILE`).

In general, we do not include any config header file from the public OT header files (headers in `include/openthread` folder). In the case where user of OT provides its own project specific config header file, we technically don't own the header file and therefore cannot expose it from OT's public header files (note that other libs can include OT public headers to use OT APIs and they may have no access to the the user given project config header).

The approach/solution we have used so far is to keep the definitions in public OT header files generic:
- In cases where the API itself is tied to certain config feature, we keep the declaration of API and document the required feature in the API description (but then don't provide the implementation in the core). Example of this are [openthread/ip6.h slaac APIs](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/bf4dae0c18dc84bb87091897d79d9b3d678fda66/include/openthread/ip6.h#L518-L575).
- For data members that are tied to some feature, we again include them and document that their use requires the conifg feature. Examples are "time sync" fields in [`otThreadLinkInfo`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/bf4dae0c18dc84bb87091897d79d9b3d678fda66/include/openthread/link.h#L56-L71), or `mFrameErrorRate` or `mMessageErrorRate` fields in [`otNeighborInfo`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/bf4dae0c18dc84bb87091897d79d9b3d678fda66/include/openthread/thread.h#L85-L109) definition.

> Also, do you think it is a good idea to add some grace period instead of immediately retrying here?

I think it's necessary. Internally in grpc there is retry logic (https://github.com/grpc/proposal/blob/master/A6-client-retries.md#retryable-status-codes) which we can use.

But for this one, at most, it'll cover 5 retries and the application layer should handle the timeout failure.

I think for the short term, we can just use that feature, but for long-term, we need to handle gRPC failure properly in application layer. In case of any kind of failure, application layer should be able to take care of them instead of letting the gRPC handling the logic like here (queueing and retrying).

def _meta_param(meta):

Some ways I could see for us to do this, with varying trade-offs, in my rough preferred order:
* Propose a new field to the `ExecutedActionMetadata` [field](https://github.com/bazelbuild/remote-apis/blob/1b16ed76965afa8bb229ba22b90745c546416443/build/bazel/remote/execution/v2/remote_execution.proto#L792) which optionally notes the execution platform of the worker. That would also mean our code could stop hard-coding that that platform is Linux.
* Define a wrapper type, either a protobuf type or a Rust type which we teach to serialize/deserialize with serde, which has a platform and an `ExecuteResponse`. Store that instead of just the `ExecuteResponse` in our lmdb.
* Use an extension field on `ExecuteResponse` to set the platform. Reset the field before we do anything else with the deserialized value (to avoid accidentally missing caches). That would be kind of hacky, because the standard proto may start using that field number at some point.

Hi @laszukdawid 

I included the tests and thanks to that I found that this exception was not working properly.

For **emd.py** it was working because the IMFs and residue were both declared inside the constructor (`self.__init__`), while in the other EMD variants, these variables were declared only inside another method (like in `def eemd(self, S, T=None, max_imf=-1):`.

Because of that, the conditional was checking for None values in a non-existent variable, the error that actually raises is `AttributeError` instead of `ValueError`.

I see 2 workarounds for this:

- For all Python modules (emd, eemd, ceemdan), transfer the IMF and residue declarations to the constructor, initializing them with None, and then leave the `get_imfs_and_residue` method as it is. So every module becomes consistent.

-  Or bring the IMF and residue declarations in **emd.py** inside the `emd` method (so it is consistent with the other modules) and  then, replace the if statement in `get_imfs_and_residue` method by a try...except statement raising an exception for the `AttributeError`. In the exception, we could print a message and raise it again, or automatically run the method to compute the IMFs and residue using some default input parameters.

I don't know which one you prefer. In my opinion, running the method automatically after the exception is a bad idea because some users may use the code blindly without worrying about the input parameters. In this commit, the solution that I am adopting is the first one, but let me know if you want me to change it.

Currently there are 3 ways SetDoc is accomplished.
* [This](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/onnx/defs/generator/defs.cc#L19) is easy to eliminate since it is taken care of in the SetDoc function itself. 
* [This](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/onnx/defs/logical/defs.cc#L175) can be hidden behind a generic macro as follows:
```
.SetDoc(GET_OP_DOC_STR(std::string(BitShift_ver11_doc) + GenerateBroadcastingDocMul())

#ifndef __ONNX_NO_DOC_STRINGS
#define GET_OP_DOC_STR(doc_str) (doc_str)
#else
#define GET_OP_DOC_STR(doc_str) ("")
#endif
```
* Finally the third way is [this](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/onnx/defs/logical/defs.cc#L28). This is a bit tricky to do in a generic way due to the way doc is constructed. Having the ```ifndef __NO_DOC_STRINGS``` inside SetDoc is not enough in this case. Surrounding SetDoc with this macro also doesn't work since it's too late. Once the 'doc' variable is used, the linker can't throw it away in the generated binary. I don't have a good solution for this one right now. Some more thought might be required.

The original thinking behind the `authorization_parameters` field was that that would be a dictionary that could be directly mapped to a query string (e.g. using `urlencode`) that would be passed the `/v2/oauth2/authorize` to get a valid token.  `session_requires_scopes` is not a recognized query param by auth, and since this is one specified be oauth2, we don't have the luxury of changing it.   If we are going to change the names parameters in the error format from what is passed into the `authorize` request, I'd suggest we also provide a method for constructing a valid authorize request from the error itself.

Additionally, while `required_scopes` is more descriptive than `scope`, adding `session` to it is a bit misleading since scopes have no relationship to sessions at all.  The consent can be granted in a different session and still satisfy the original token's authorization needs.

great question, I wasn't sure myself at first what the difference is between np.frombuffer and np.fromiter, but based on this:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/58091879/whats-the-difference-between-frombuffer-and-fromiter-in-numpywhy-and-when-to-us
and docs here:
https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.frombuffer.html
https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.fromiter.html

it looks like frombuffer uses the existing ctypes array whereas fromiter allocates a new array, so I think it's better to use frombuffer in order to reduce memory usage and data copying.

```
 numpy.frombuffer(buffer, dtype=float, count=-1, offset=0)
    Interpret a buffer as a 1-dimensional array.
```
```
 numpy.fromiter(iterable, dtype, count=-1)
    Create a new 1-dimensional array from an iterable object.
```

I don't like the existing behaviour because we can't differentiate a close from a timeout. So I'd say either stick to the default socket/posix behaviour or raise an exception for everything.

The default posix way would be choice 2 if I understand correctly. Raise socket.timeout and return b"" on close.

However, as we are on application level I could also imagine raising an exception for both cases. Not sure what others think about this but it might be the most user friendly. You simply tell boofuzz to receive after a test case and if anything goes wrong (close/timeout/abort/reset) you get an exception.
On the other hand, I know some of my targets close the connection if the received data was invalid. In that case the target didn't crash but boofuzz would raise an exception. That might be a bit inconvenient.

I also just found the session option `check_data_received_each_request`. It seems this option is the reason for both close and timeout returning b"". If we switch to exceptions, we'd have to catch them here and depending on the setup maybe re-raise?

Right now I favour choice 2 as anyone with basic knowledge about sockets will understand what's going on. Also it looks like that approach could easily be adapted to the current `check_data_received_each_request` behaviour.

A few usability notes (context in GDAL [RFC #4](https://gdal.org/development/rfc/rfc4_geolocate.html)):

* `kwargs` should disallow the use of `METHOD=GEOLOC_ARRAY` and `SRC_GEOLOC_ARRAY`.
* Is the intention to support `PIXEL_STEP` and `LINE_STEP` via `kwargs`? These seem like key parameters for some users. `PIXEL_OFFSET` and `LINE_OFFSET` seem less useful, but worth supporting for completeness.
* It is possible to assign a geolocation array to the target image using the `DST_GEOLOC_ARRAY` transformer option. If `kwargs` is passed along correctly it seems like this should work if the caller configures things correctly, but it may be worth thinking about whether or not this should be more officially supported, or explicitly disallowed.
* It is possible for the input image to have `GEOLOCATION` metadata set. This is a special metadata domain like `RPC`, and normally points to an external dataset. Another useful pattern is to construct a geolocation array, attach `GEOLOCATION` metadata, and omit the `*_DATASET` metadata items. This dataset can then be applied to any dataset of the right shape without having to manage geolocation metadata on the primary image dataset. Is Rasterio's stance that this metadata will be ignored, and users must manually load it and craft an appropriate call to `reproject()`?
* Rasterio must be able to read and write `GEOLOCATION` metadata, otherwise the library cannot be used to craft an image whose geolocation is provided by a geolocation array. I think I confirmed this works, but IMO should be intentionally tested.
* A geolocation can include an optional `Z_BAND`, although I suspect this is ignored by the geolocation array transformer, and exists to provide a mechanism to include elevation for use elsewhere.

A merge conflict is a much more informative thing to give them than a rolled-back repository in that case, and doesn't touch their work. I'm starting to doubt the "two kinds of hyperparameter optimization estimators" I laid out on line 6.

After our conversation the other day, it sounds like there are (at least) 3.

1. Static search: All combinations to be tried are specified up front. The full dataset (or a CV split of the full dataset) is used on each training call.
2. Adaptive search: Regardless of exactly which data is used (CV split of full dataset, or a batch), the salient feature of adaptive search is that models are prioritized according to past performance. 
3. Incremental search: Regardless of how models are chosen for training (static or adaptive), the salient feature of Incremental search is that models are trained on *batches* of data passed to `_parital_fit`. The underlying estimator *must* implement `partial_fit`.

Our current implementation is a mix of 2 and 3. Does that framing accord with other's thoughts? Can we think of a clear way to explain that? The docs below do a decent job I think. This line, though, I think emphasizes adaptive too much given the framing above (you have a large dataset).

This means optimisation is now off by default, which isn't good.  As well as making the code slower and (most likely) larger, some compiler warnings only work if optimisation is on.

The option which is really needed here is `-Iinclude` since without that the headers won't be found.  The others are desirable as defaults, but all are reasonable to override - you might want a different optimisation setting; you might be using a compiler which doesn't understand `-Wmissing-prototypes`.

We could just leave `CFLAGS` as it is and only have the `override CPPFLAGS += -Iinclude` change, but perhaps it'd be better to move `-Iinclude` in a new variable (`INCLUDES`, or `REQUIRED_CPPFLAGS` or something), and leave these as-is.

3.  _process_executor_events method being called followed by hearbeat method which detects external killing of task, if TI.state is queued and event_buffer key status is success/failed

Just that it makes it easy to make the same argument optional in the calling func.

`interval` comes all the way from `stackdriver.stats_exporter.new_stats_exporter`, if we used `DEFAULT_INTERVAL` here we'd have to either have to use it as the default value in the callers (which means importing it in other modules) or make two separate calls, e.g.

```python
def caller_in_another_module(interval=None):
    ...
    if interval is None:
        task = PeriodicTask()
    else:
        task = PeriodicTask(interval=interval)
```

Using `None` everywhere is convenient, but there are a few big drawbacks: it's less readable since you can't tell the defaults by looking at the signature, you can't change the default value to something other than `None` without changing all the callers too, and it doesn't work for arguments that can actually have `None` as their value.

`checked_cast()` doesn't work with `Union` because `Union` is not a `Type`, so `Type[T]` doesn't match.

For bounded-length tuples, this is achievable:
```python
def checked_cast_to_union2(target_types: Tuple[Type[T_A], Type[T_B]], value: Any) -> Union[T_A, T_B]:
    if not isinstance(value, target_types):
        raise AssertionError()

    return value
```

I don't know of a way to make `target_types: Tuple[Type, ...]` work and connect the types it contains to the types inside the `Union` result.

Given that we're somewhat unlikely to have unions of more than like 3 or so things, I'm fine with adding `checked_cast_to_union2()` and in the future potentially adding a `checked_cast_to_union3()` function that takes a 3-tuple as necessary.

The longest valid chain wins, where "longest" means "has the most blocks."  Over long enough time scales, this will also be the chain that has the most burns.

The problem we are trying to solve is to decide which of a set of valid chains is the "best."  The behavior we want is for the "best" chain to be the chain that an overwhelming majority of miners compete to produce blocks on -- we want miners to reap the rewards of making progress on the already-best chain whenever possible.  We achieve this end by rewarding miners for building off of the chain with the most blocks, since blocks are a proxy for Stacks economic activity.

An alternative is to consider a chain to be "best" only if it is valid and has the most cumulative burns.  We don't encourage miners to build off of the most widely-used chain by allowing anyone to make a large burn and produce a shorter chain that is considered "better" while invalidating a lot of already-confirmed blocks.  Such chains should not be considered "better" precisely because doing so would enable this unintuitive behavior, and would allow malicious whales to reduce the system's liveness and hurt honest miners.

Nevertheless, the longest valid chain is also guaranteed to be the chain with the most burns over time, since an honest majority coalition of miners will all compete to be selected to be the next minder by burning higher BTC than their peers.  The side-effect of competing to mine on the longest chain is that the sum of the burns on that chain will also (over time) exceed the sums of burns on minority forks.

The reason we can get away with only considering chain length for quality is because PoB miners enjoy two advantages over PoW miners:  access to a global clock (via the PoW chain's block arrivals), and an arbitrarily-low barrier to entry for participating in the protocol.  In PoW chains, the act of mining serves two purposes:  to start new epochs, and to enhance chain quality.  As such, a PoW chain's fork-choice rule considers the chain with the most valid proof-of-work to be the best chain.

In PoB chains, the act of mining only enhances chain quality, since the mechanism that starts and ends epochs is outside the PoB miners' control.  This means that chain quality is not directly dependent on the amount of tokens burned; it's only dependent on the number of blocks produced on the same fork.  The only way for a PoB minority fork to overtake the majority fork is for the majority of miners (by burn) to produce blocks on the minority fork until its length exceeds the majority fork.  Since miners are competing in each epoch to be selected as the next miner (meaning that they burn a non-zero amount of tokens), over time the new majority fork will have more burns than the old majority fork.

It's important to remember that using chain length as the quality metric still allows the system to tolerate deep forks (such as to recover from a catastrophic network crash).  However, this enables two nice properties of chain reorganizations: (1) anyone can "see a reorg coming" because all blocks produced are anchored to the same history on the burn chain, and (2) deep forks take a long time to carry out.  These are particularly useful properties for a young PoB chain, because it increases the barrier to entry for a powerful malicious miner who can afford to out-burn a fledgling chain's miners.  I think this will prove useful for app-chains, for example.

> Just thinking that 64 characters is rather long if this If this
> automatic session name gets exposed in the UI anywhere.

It is exposed in the table view, but I have been thinking of removing
that. All the examples that I’ve seen have either a hash or an
incrementing integer their, neither of which is useful. If we remove
that, then we don’t have to have “session groups” or “trials” or
anything in the UI at all.

Note also that these strings in the table view are already truncated
(`text-overflow: ellipsis;`), so truncating from 64- to 32-character
strings in the data wouldn’t actually make an observable difference.
Also, we really shouldn’t shove *either* 32-character hex strings or
64-character hex strings into users’ faces; cutting the length in half
doesn’t actually solve the problem imho. So, given that each session
gropu name appears on disk exactly once across all event files (unlike
tag names, for instance), I’m inclined to keep it as is.

Note any time a shard that extends `x-{osx,linux}-{27,37}` needs to change its `env` value, it will need to re-call everything that the env originally had.

For example:
```yml
env: 
  - *env
  - *x-py27
  - *x-osx-ssl
  - foo=bar
```

As opposed to something like:

```yml
env:
  - *x-osx-27-shard-env
  - foo=bar
```

--

For Pants, we change the `env` so much in `.travis.yml` that this wouldn't be acceptable, so we workaround it by exploiting the fact that Travis allows multiple env vars to be one entry, so we create entries like
```yml
env:
  - &py27_osx_env: VAL1=foo VAL2=bar
```

Because YAML does not allow reading an alias (`*unpack_alias`) the same time you're defining a new anchor `&my_new_anchor`), we have unfortunate duplication of certain env vars.

--

We do not seem to be extending env much here, so I think your approach is better.

I'm concerned we can't/shouldn't try to be lazy across WDL tasks; if you write a file in one task and pass it to a bunch of scattered tasks, you don't expect the write to re-happen in all those scattered tasks. You want it to have happened once in the initial scattering task, even though the file might not make it into a command line until the scattered tasks try to use it.

But at the same time, we want a task to be able to return the result of its child tasks, subject to some WDL post-processing, which can't happen while the task itself is running.

So it sounds like every WDL task might really need to be two jobs: we need a follow-on job (or a final child whose result is returned) to run the WDL code that depends on the child jobs' results.

The line 276 with `bash -i` is also a way to force the interactive shell, according to `man bash`:

...
OPTIONS
       All  of  the  single-character  shell  options  documented in the description of the set builtin command can be used as options when the shell is
       invoked.  In addition, bash interprets the following options when it is invoked:

       -c        If the -c option is present, then commands are read from the first non-option argument command_string.  If there  are  arguments  after
                 the  command_string,  the  first argument is assigned to $0 and any remaining arguments are assigned to the positional parameters.  The
                 assignment to $0 sets the name of the shell, which is used in warning and error messages.
       -i        If the -i option is present, the shell is interactive.
...

So basically the `set -i` is moved to the options of invoking bash, which is compatible with both bash 4.3 and 4.4.

If you put 'def _plot3d' in one module, then import it in another module, any decent IDE will complain that you're accessing a private variable. The leading underscore should indicate that the function (/class/module/method/variable) is only intended to be used within the current scope (i.e. the same module/module/parent module/class/module for function/class/module/method/variable, respectively). 

It will break absolutely nothing to use `def _plot3d`, it's just not conventional. Well, I guess it might break the codacy check, but whatever.

If we're going to use an underscore though, I'd rather underscore the module, because it's better form.

With the current redistribution logic I think that change could make it unsafe to call `set_max_in_flight()` with messages in flight. Right now redistribution avoids giving positive RDY to a connection with a message in flight, so something like the following could happen:

1. Let max-in-flight be  > `len(conns)`.
2. With a message in flight on conn `c`, reduce m-i-f but  keep it >= `len(conns)`.
3. `_redistribute_rdy_state()` sets RDY 0 on all connections, including `c`.
4. Because `c` has something in flight, this redistribution round leaves `c` at RDY 0.
5. Subsequent redistribution rounds see m-i-f >= `len(conns)` and so don't redistribute RDY, leaving `c` starved.

We could make the simplification you suggest if we allow redistribution to give RDY to a conn with something in flight:
```
--- a/nsq/reader.py
+++ b/nsq/reader.py
@@ -720,7 +720,7 @@ class Reader(Client):
             # We also don't attempt to avoid the connections who previously might have had RDY 1
             # because it would be overly complicated and not actually worth it (ie. given enough
             # redistribution rounds it doesn't matter).
-            possible_conns = [c for c in conns if not (c.in_flight or c.rdy)]
+            possible_conns = [c for c in conns if not c.rdy]
             while possible_conns and available_rdy:
                 available_rdy -= 1
                 conn = possible_conns.pop(random.randrange(len(possible_conns)))
```
which (I think) is fine from a correctness standpoint and will at worst increase the variance in how long it takes to service all connections equally.

Sounds good, will send an error in both cases. In case this case becomes important eventually, the general flow in the situation I'm thinking of would be:
* User Ctrl-C's
* The main thread (thread that the driver program is running on) receives the error, and begins to exit while doing on async get
* The client worker's close method is called
* The data_client's close method is called
* `None` is dumped into request_queue
* The data_main for loop exits without error (sentinel `None` hit) and goes through the , possible with some outstanding async callbacks

Basically, a Ctrl-C to the driver thread will still lead to a graceful shutdown of the data_main thread

Why remove all this logic? There is a reason for this specific implementation: it was here in order to retrieve _all_ forums that match a specific set of permissions for a specific user. On the other hand `ForumPermissionChecker` instances work on a per-forum basis.

As a rule of thumb, the `ForumPermissionHandler` class defines logics to filter lists of forums for a specific user based on permissions while the `ForumPermissionChecker` class defines logics to tell whether a specific forum has a specific set of permissions granted for a specific user.

That's why I'd prefer if the new `authenticated_user` rule could be implemented as part of the original implementation of this `_get_forums_for_user` method. Otherwise you're forced to use the checker on each forum instance (like you did), and this generates _a lot_ of SQL queries.

For example, on my test project, displaying the index results in about 10 SQL queries in the current _master_ branch. With your modifications I end up with **36** SQL queries to display the index for an authenticated user, which not acceptable.

So here is what I can suggest:

1. you introduce the new `authenticated_user` logic to the `_get_forums_for_user` by complying to the existing strategy
2. and you introduce the new `authenticated_user` logic to the `ForumPermissionChecker` too

> What do other formatters do here when running on windows vs. POSIX?
>
> Should we (if found) leave Windows line terminators alone? (i.e. "\r\n")

I do not have a windows machine at my disposal but I tested a few formatters from different languages:

* Yapf: Leaves a completely empty file as is,  a whitespace only file without newline gets converted to `\n`, a file with a single newline (`\n` or `\r\n`) is preserved the way it was before and a file with multiple newlines is truncated to a `\n` (even if the newlines were `\r\n`).
* Prettier (for JS): Defaults to `\n` for each platform.
* Rustfmt (for Rust): Seems to convert an empty file to `\n`, otherwise identical to yapf.

Personally I think it would be the best compromise is to leave a completely empty file as is and a file with non-newline whitespace should be converted to a completely empty file. For all other files we can infer the line ending and preserve it.

Here is a patch that seems to work for me, but double check that it looks sane to you too. Plus you might be able to get rid of some of the duplication.

```diff
diff --git a/tests/test_multiprocess.py b/tests/test_multiprocess.py
index a2a1dbf..845cc3d 100644
--- a/tests/test_multiprocess.py
+++ b/tests/test_multiprocess.py
@@ -32,12 +32,17 @@ class TestMultiProcessDeprecation(unittest.TestCase):
     def tearDown(self):
         del os.environ['prometheus_multiproc_dir']
         del os.environ['PROMETHEUS_MULTIPROC_DIR']
+        values.ValueClass = MutexValue
         shutil.rmtree(self.tempdir)

     def test_deprecation_warning(self):
         os.environ['prometheus_multiproc_dir'] = self.tempdir
         with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as w:
-            get_value_class()
+            values.ValueClass = get_value_class()
+            registry = CollectorRegistry()
+            collector = MultiProcessCollector(registry, self.tempdir)
+            Counter('c', 'help', registry=None)
+
             assert os.environ['PROMETHEUS_MULTIPROC_DIR'] == self.tempdir
             assert len(w) == 1
             assert issubclass(w[-1].category, DeprecationWarning)
@@ -47,8 +52,12 @@ class TestMultiProcessDeprecation(unittest.TestCase):
         os.environ['prometheus_multiproc_dir'] = 'should not be picked'
         os.environ['PROMETHEUS_MULTIPROC_DIR'] = self.tempdir
         with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as w:
-            get_value_class()
-            assert len(w) == 0
+            values.ValueClass = get_value_class()
+            registry = CollectorRegistry()
+            collector = MultiProcessCollector(registry, self.tempdir)
+            Counter('c', 'help', registry=None)
+
+            assert len(w) == 1


 class TestMultiProcess(unittest.TestCase):
```

So in this implementation the default diffing library we use is difflib.unified_diff. We found that gives us the most easily digestible format for humans. There are some issues with it though, specifically around how it handles ordering with JSON templates. Also I'm pretty sure it's only python 3 (but is that really a problem? 😛 ).

Note that a feature based on difflib.unified_diff was added upstream in #212 but then reverted again later in #225. 

For those 2 reasons we also wanted to provide an option for a much more robust option if the user required it.

We did look into using DeepDiff, however, found that dictdiffer provided extra information in a format that could more easily be parsed. YMMV.  If DeepDiff can be configured to give us a friendly diff format similar to difflib.unified_diff, that would be the best of both worlds!

Examples Below:

difflib.unified_diff.
```diff
test-test: --- remote_template
+++ local_template
@@ -23,10 +23,14 @@
         - Key: Project
           Value: GitOps
+        - Key: Foo
+          Value: Bar
 
 Outputs:
   InstanceArn:
     Value: !Ref MyInstance
+  AvailabilityZone:
+    Value: !GetAtt MyInstance.AvailabilityZone
 
 # vim: set ft=yaml:
```

dictdiffer
```
[aws:personal1] /src $ sceptre --dir=test --var-file=/env/common-env.yaml --var-file=/env/test.yaml diff test.yaml 
["test-test: [('add', 'Resources.MyInstance.Properties.Tags', [(6, {'Key': 'Foo', 'Value': 'Bar'})]), ('remove', 'Resources.MyInstance.Properties', [('InstanceType', 'm1.small')])]"]
```

DeepDiff
```
[aws:personal1] /src $ sceptre --dir=test --var-file=/env/common-env.yaml --var-file=/env/test.yaml diff test.yaml 
test-test: Item root['Resources']['MyInstance']['Properties']['InstanceType'] removed from dictionary.
Item root['Resources']['MyInstance']['Properties']['Tags'][6] added to iterable.
```

I agree with the guideline. As OT does not provide locks on state/variables read/write, OT APIs which need access to OT state/variables should not be called in context other than OT OS context. For an OT API to be used in other context, it should be OT variables independent.

So I think the best place to handle Enh-ACK and frame counter is at radio layer if it supports `OT_RADIO_CAPS_TRANSMIT_SEC`. It would be easier to maintain atomicity and accurate time control there than at OT stack which involves context switch and maybe tasklet scheduling. Otherwise, OT `SubMac` provides an alternative to it as a last resort at the cost of possibility of not meeting the criterial to send out ACK within AIFS(192us).

Or do you think we should force Radio to support `OT_RADIO_CAPS_TRANSMIT_SEC` if build version is OT1.2?

BTW, there are some discussion in the Thread group regarding the frame counter used in Enh-ACK(Check this comment, https://threadgroup.atlassian.net/browse/SPEC-939?focusedCommentId=48001). It tries to use the frame counter in received frame with some variance in nonce generation to avoid reusing the same key material. This approach is still under discussion. If this approach is adopted by spec, then it will be easier to implement Enh-ACK as it does not rely on the transmit frame counter.

Macros are essentially string-processing on the text of the source file (which is why they provide so few guarantees), so if something depends on a function that could be derived from some code, you can pass in the name of the function and use it both to select the function's arguments and use it as the string that gets passed to `dlopen`. I can't give an example because I haven't thought through the solution (and I also don't know the preprocessor instructions for concatenating strings to build the code you want), but you may be able to apply a little creativity here. The macro doesn't necessary have to call the function: it might just construct the function and the actual argument passing might happen on the output of the macro (making var-args unnecessary).

The point of this is to be simple, so if trying to write this is getting out of hand, then it's better not to. Basically, you want to find any way to reduce the code duplication (a.k.a. "boilerplate") so that what we see in each function is pretty much just passing through the function arguments by name. If there has to be boilerplate, making it regular enough to edit by "replace all" could be acceptable, because under certain conditions that's not prone to error. But anything that has intricate invariants to maintain is dangerous.

I'll think about it, too. This can't be the first time someone has needed to glue a large codebase accessible through `dlopen`.

I think this is how it used to work, but the problem is that the `eval` step needs to check, whether the `train` step (which is running in a thread) is done, not the other way around. So the logic is:
- start train step in a thread
- do eval (10 timesteps as per rollout_frag_len setting)
- check if train is done
-- no? -> do another 10 steps
-- yes? -> return eval results immediately, such that the next call to `train` doesn't have to be blocked by a still running eval step

Note that we probably should not use the phases "complete decoupling" as this is misleading: We still need some form of coupling as we have to synch weights before each train+eval step from local worker to all evaluation workers. It's merely the duration of the eval step that we more closely align with the train duration in this PR such that it feels like no one has to wait for the other one anymore.

I'll add some example settings to the docs as well and change the title of this PR to clarify this. I hope this explains the logic.

Good question! I guess I chose this location to change the allocator as this was the test that ran out of memory on the BuildKite agent. And the reason that happens is because the tests in `test_mnist`, which load and use a TF model, get executed before `test_model`, allowing TF to allocate almost all remaining GPU memory. From that point onwards, any allocations by `cupy` or `torch` run the risk of triggering an OOM error.

I agree with you that this is just papering over the cracks and that the most sensible way to fix this would be to implement a resource manager for TF ops. Unfortunately, there appears to be no [way to programmatically coerce TF into releasing its memory pool](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/36465) 😭 - The only time it happens is when the host process gets terminated and the GPU driver (presumably) releases its GPU resources.

The only other alternative we have is to selectively execute TF tests in a child process. But then we will break the `pytest` machinery that captures the test result and its `stdout/err` output. And there seems to be [no portable and fool-proof ](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/48234032/run-py-test-test-in-different-process)way of executing specific `pytest` tests in child processes.

Why is this test relevant to the change?

disable_reconfig is used to avoid loops while rendering:
- there are hooks to change in an entry which generates a call to the backend to update the entity
- when an entity is deleted, the way the UI framework works is that it copies the value from the next entry to the previous one (and eventually deletes the last entry). This process generated many calls to the backend, and basically put the system in a loop.
- to avoid it, we ignore changes in the entries, while they are being rendered. We only care about changes when they are triggered manually by the user.
- disable_reconfig is the flag for implementing this logic.

1. [Asio's primary support](https://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_73_0/doc/html/boost_asio/overview/core/buffers.html) is for e.g. [`mutable_buffer`](https://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_71_0/doc/html/boost_asio/reference/buffer.html) which is a pointer and a size, not vector.

2. I don't think we need `size()` though, do we? We weren't using it so far (given `std::unique_ptr` sufficed for our usage), and even after the change, I see
    ```
    flatbuffers::GetRoot<protocol::RegisterClientReply>(reply.data());
    ```
    which still ignores the size. I might be missing something though.

3. Vector has additional initialization overhead (I'm not referring to allocation) compared to a plain dynamic array.

4. I agree `std::unique_ptr<uint8_t[]> *` isn't pretty, but `std::vector<uint8_t> *` is the same thing with size/capacity added. The double-nullity is nice to avoid, but you can avoid that with a reference rather than a pointer. In fact, the Google style guide (which I don't really defer to personally) [says the same thing](https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html): _"required (non-nullable) output and input/output parameters should usually be references"_.

Personally my biggest concern though is that `vector` is semantically meant to be a random-access container and not a buffer of raw memory, so it ends up being somewhat misleading IMO. But that said, I don't feel too strongly about it, so if you disagree despite these feel free to keep it.

We allow for both comma-delimited lists and multi-option usages. To summarize my views on the two modes of use in two points:

1. Multi-option use is very explicit, allows for more robust and complex parsing, but is also more onerous for users. Comma-delimited lists are very easy and lightweight, but more limited.
2. They're two equally good APIs for users, and should be tailored to specific use-cases, not one-size-fits-all.

GCS and Transfer both treat keywords as a comma-delimited lists, which is worth keeping on our radar. That's partly driven by a service behavior, in that Transfer actually requires keywords for endpoint search to be provided as a string field, with comma-delimited semantics service-side.

<details><summary>GCS tangent</summary>
GCS is a little bit inbetween, accepting a list of strings, but then needing to serialize that and send it to Transfer (to reflect the data there) as a comma-delimited list. So even though GCS might accept `["foo,bar", "baz"]`, it can't mean anything different from `"foo,bar,baz"`.
</details>

Whether or not cross-service consistency is a goal (I would vaguely like it but also don't think it's terribly realistic to expect it at present), I think the meaning of keywords for search and retrieval is better aligned with the more lightweight comma-delimited usage.
Regarding the other flavor of consistency -- that within a command or API -- I'm not sure that it makes sense to align the mode of use for `--keyword` with `--administrator` (to stick with that example) because they're two different kinds of data.

I don't think a default for strategy makes sense. This is the most important value for tuning the schema to achieve good linkage. We should enforce users to take it seriously. Also, there is a dependency between clk length, number of features and strategy. A fixed strategy, which doesn't take the number of features into account is pretty useless most of the time.

Furthermore, for all other default values, we ignore what's written in the schema spec, but have something either hardcoded in code (e.g. schema.py line 184), default argument values (e.g. field_formats.py line 92, 93), or some hidden configurable variables (e.g. field_formats.py line 85,86). Adding default values from schema extraction, ahem, celebrates diversity and is thus in line with our company values, however, it also might be the last straw that tips the next review over the edge of despair. Maybe we should start spreading links to the EAP around... just in case.

On a more serious note, we should rethink all this defaults business. I don't like, that:
- the defaults are spread throughout the code base
- there is no guarantee that the default in the schema correlates to the default in the code
- the user doesn't actually know the exact details of the schema used to create the CLKs. This makes it unnecessarily hard to reproduce results...
But that should probably go into a separate issue.

Nevertheless, instead of this hardcoded default extraction, we should consider doing it like this https://python-jsonschema.readthedocs.io/en/stable/faq/#why-doesn-t-my-schema-s-default-property-set-the-default-on-my-instance. This way, it would not just be more elegant, but also, it would create a json structure with all values set, which we could then feed back to the user -> the effective schema. Wow.

At the risk of harping on this very minor point too much, I might agree with keeping it in the parser tests if the fact we're calling `load` on Augeas had to do with the code in `parser.py` but I don't think it does. In `configurator.py`, we're reaching into the parser object and calling `parser.aug.load()` directly. I think the only code in `parser.py` that this function is indirectly testing is that the parser has an `aug` attribute which is tested plenty of other places.

I completely agree with you that we want a configurator test asserting that we properly reload the config using the correct interface after a recovery routine, but I think this is that test. We're checking that the implementation of `recovery_routine` in the configurator is properly reloading the parser with its currently existing interfaces. When the parser's interface changes, we'll need to update the code in `configurator.py` to use those interfaces as well as the tests for that configurator code. We'll also probably want a parser test in the future that tests that the common `load()` interface properly calls `load()` on Augeas, but I don't think this test needs to be coupled to the configurator code.

For those reasons, I personally think it makes sense to just have a single test for reloading during `recovery_routine` and for this test to exist in `configurator_reverter_test.py`.

If you disagree with me, I'd kind of like to know why, but I don't think it's worth blocking this PR on this minor point. If you still prefer to keep the test here after my additional explanation of how I'm thinking about this, let me know and I'll merge this PR as is.

This was failing in CI on Windows only. If you look at the `pytest_report` (attached to this PR), these were the tests failing with the error message

```
commitizen.exceptions.GitCommandError: fatal: ambiguous argument 'HEAD': unknown revision or path not in the working tree.
```

Specifically,

- `test_bump_pre_commit_changelog`
- `test_bump_pre_commit_changelog_fails_always`

This signaled to me no commit had been made, so I captured `sys.out` on the tests and indeed found that to be the case. This is easily reproducible in Command Prompt:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/59346180/197348149-f339f46e-4ba9-49f7-808e-2d2c9f2e2db5.png)

In my commit message on `9d40`, I explain what's happening:

> On Windows, `Popen` with `shell=True` reads the `COMSPEC` environment
> variable to determine the shell to run, which defaults to Command
> Prompt (see
> https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.run).
> For backwards-compatibility for older OS utility scripts, `COMSPEC`
> should not be changed on Windows.
> 
> Command Prompt requires double-quotes instead of single-quotes. See
> 
>   - https://stackoverflow.com/questions/25534995/escape-double-quotes-in-git-config-from-cmd)
>   - https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#converting-an-argument-sequence-to-a-string-on-windows
> 
> Note that although the commitizen shell `scripts` shebangs have
> been changed to a portable variety (i.e. `#!/usr/bin/env sh`),
> which runs Git Bash on Windows when Git is installed (i.e.
> the `sh.exe` executable that ships with Git for Windows),
> `subprocess.Popen()` opens a child process from the active
> running shell (i.e. via `CreateProcess()`). Hence, it will
> still read `COMSPEC` and run a Command Prompt shell.

https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/4eba1a098698f301e2996fba23a72927fb5d07a2/rdflib/graph.py#L2036-L2041

Because if it is not a node it will be converted to URIRef here:

https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/4eba1a098698f301e2996fba23a72927fb5d07a2/rdflib/graph.py#L342-L345

And actually it should also never be anything other than the Default Graph (in this case identified by none), an IRI or a blank node:

https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#section-dataset

> 
> An RDF dataset is a collection of [RDF graphs](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph), and comprises:
> 
>  - Exactly one default graph, being an [RDF graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph). The default graph does not have a name and may be empty.
>  - Zero or more named graphs. Each named graph is a pair consisting of an [IRI](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-iri) or a blank node (the graph name), and an [RDF graph](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/#dfn-rdf-graph). Graph names are unique within an RDF dataset.

> Please note that this does not ensure we end up with the same python libraries in docker - in Docker, we don't install requirements_test.txt, so there are less constraints on the dependencies.

> However, this PR can be used to e.g. use pip-compile to compile a list of dependency constraints, and use these in the docker image build to achieve the same dependencies.

Many of these problems (and pins) will be gone when we upgrade CI to Python 3.8, and it will become much more feasible to make the environments similar / the same without any pins of core dependencies.

There's some subtle difference in whether we think of this as "an internal doc, posted publicly" or "a public explanation of our process"

I'm onboard with being transparent. If we're being transparent we can use language that's accessible to the readers to whom we're being transparent. E.g. if it's an internal only doc then terms like `fbcode` which reference a particular file path make sense. If we're being transparent so external contributors can see and understand our process, it seems slightly nicer to write out things like "facebook internal code" instead of referencing the directory name or internal abbreviations.

In some cases a statement like "We should/will/must check for..." might feel more inclusive than an imperative command directed at the reader ("Check for...") so that there's less of an implication that the reader is a FB employee.

though, I do notice examples of the imperative form in the referenced maintainer guides.

I'd advocate still simplifying these 3 lines:

```python
for missing_init in identify_missing_init_files([target_workdir]):
  Path(missing_init).touch()
```

Couple reasons I'm advocating for this:
1. Consistency with how we handle missing `__init__.py` in the rest of the codebase.
1. Less code.
1. Makes the `Path()` creation simpler. `identify_missing_init_files()` will return back the whole file path, so you don't need `os.path.join` anymore.

--

As an aside, if you were to keep this original version, you could use `pathlib.Path`'s use of the `/` operator in place of `os.path.join`, like this:

```python
(Path(root) / dirname / '__init__.py').touch()
```

The syntax is supposed to mirror how directories are in posix systems, e.g. `hello/world/test.py`.

import modin.pandas as pd

shapes = [

]

Just to be clear: This particular action only build the source distribution, not any binary wheel. It's purpose is for people that have a machine that is incompatible with any of the pre-compiled wheels.

I assume that you want to add `--force-bundled-libcapnp` in order to distribute the sources files of the upstream Capn'Proto release, so that they don't need to be downloaded separately? In principle, that sounds okay (but not critical), but I don't believe this is something that has ever happened in the past, and I don't believe that adding `--force-bundled-libcapnp` will achieve this. This flag will download and compile Capt'nProto, but the source code will still not be copied into the source distribution (I just tested this). As such, in it's current form this flag is useless for the source distribution.

Note that when building the binary wheel distribution, the flag is relevant. But I believe it still doesn't need to be added, because none of the CI images used here include Capt'nProto, and hence the build system will automatically build from source. Even if that wasn't the case, `cibuildwheel` would automatically repair the wheel to include the system-level Capt'nProto binaries into the wheel (that's the magic if this, it all happens automatically).
